cheatSheet:
## Design Patterns Cheat Sheet

### Creational Patterns
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Singleton | One instance globally | Config, Logger, DB pool |
| Factory Method | Create without specifying class | Multiple product types |
| Abstract Factory | Family of related objects | Cross-platform UI |
| Builder | Step-by-step construction | Complex objects, fluent API |
| Prototype | Clone existing objects | Expensive creation |

### Structural Patterns
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Adapter | Convert interface | Legacy integration |
| Decorator | Add behavior dynamically | Streams, middleware |
| Facade | Simplify complex subsystem | API wrappers |
| Proxy | Control access | Lazy loading, caching |
| Composite | Tree structures | File systems, UI components |
| Bridge | Separate abstraction from impl | Cross-platform |

### Behavioral Patterns
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Observer | Notify on state change | Event systems, MVC |
| Strategy | Swap algorithms | Payment, sorting, auth |
| Command | Encapsulate request | Undo/redo, queues |
| Template Method | Define skeleton | Frameworks, hooks |
| Iterator | Traverse collection | Custom collections |
| State | Change behavior with state | Workflow, FSM |
| Chain of Responsibility | Pass request along chain | Middleware, logging |

### SOLID Principles
- **S**ingle Responsibility: One class, one reason to change
- **O**pen/Closed: Open for extension, closed for modification
- **L**iskov Substitution: Subtypes must be substitutable
- **I**nterface Segregation: Many specific interfaces > one general
- **D**ependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions, not concretions

### Key Relationships
- Factory vs Builder: Factory = one step, Builder = multi-step
- Strategy vs State: Strategy = client chooses, State = auto transition
- Adapter vs Facade: Adapter = 1:1 interface, Facade = simplifies many
- Decorator vs Proxy: Decorator = adds behavior, Proxy = controls access

### Anti-Patterns to Avoid
- God Object (does everything)
- Spaghetti Code (no structure)
- Golden Hammer (one solution for all)
- Premature Optimization
- Copy-Paste Programming

resources.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable OO Software
resources.author: Gang of Four

plan.overview: Master the 15 essential design patterns every software engineer needs for interviews and production code.
plan.skippedTopics: Rare patterns like Flyweight, Memento, Visitor — they appear in <1% of interviews.

sessions.title: SOLID Principles
objectives: Explain all 5 SOLID principles with code examples
Identify SOLID violations in existing code
Refactor code to comply with SOLID

activities.description: Refactor a God class into SOLID-compliant services
Identify which SOLID principle each code snippet violates

reviewQuestions: What's the difference between SRP and ISP?:::SRP is about classes having one reason to change (cohesion). ISP is about interfaces — clients should not depend on methods they don't use (coupling at the interface boundary). A class handling both user CRUD and email sending violates SRP. An interface forcing SimplePrinter to implement fax() violates ISP.

How does DIP enable unit testing?:::By depending on abstractions instead of concrete classes, you can inject mock implementations during testing. OrderService accepts a Database interface — in tests you pass InMemoryDatabase, no real DB needed.

Give an example of LSP violation and how to fix it.:::Square extends Rectangle: setting width must also change height, breaking Rectangle's contract. Fix: make both implement Shape interface independently, don't use inheritance.
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify SOLID violations in any codebase and refactor them correctly

sessions.title: Singleton Pattern
objectives: Implement thread-safe Singleton in Java and Python
Compare enum, double-checked locking, and holder idiom
Articulate when Singleton is harmful and alternatives

activities.description: Implement all three Java Singleton approaches and verify thread safety
Refactor a Singleton to use Dependency Injection instead

reviewQuestions: Why is Singleton considered an anti-pattern?:::It introduces global mutable state making tests order-dependent. Violates SRP by managing its own lifecycle AND business logic. Hides dependencies. Modern alternative: DI containers (Spring, NestJS) manage single-instance lifecycle without drawbacks.

How do you make Singleton thread-safe in Java?:::Three approaches: (1) Enum singleton — JVM guaranteed. (2) Double-checked locking — volatile + synchronized, checks twice. (3) Bill Pugh Holder — static inner class, lazy-loaded by classloader.

Can Singleton be broken?:::Yes: (1) Reflection — setAccessible(true) on private constructor. (2) Serialization — creates new instance (fix: readResolve()). (3) Cloning. Enum singleton prevents all three.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement thread-safe Singleton, explain trade-offs, and argue for DI alternatives

sessions.title: Factory Pattern
objectives: Implement Simple Factory, Registry Factory, and Abstract Factory
Know when to use each variant
Explain Factory's relationship with OCP and DIP

activities.description: Build a notification system with Simple Factory
Extend to Registry Factory — add new channel without modifying existing code
Implement Abstract Factory for UI theme rendering

reviewQuestions: Simple vs Abstract Factory?:::Simple creates one product type via conditional logic. Abstract creates families of related objects that must be consistent (DarkThemeFactory creates DarkButton + DarkInput as a matching set).
How does Registry Factory achieve OCP?:::Switch-based factory requires modifying the switch to add types (violates OCP). Registry uses a map — new types call register(). Factory code never changes.
Factory vs Builder?:::Factory: the TYPE varies (email vs SMS). Builder: the CONFIGURATION of one type varies (HTTP request with optional headers, body, timeout).
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement all Factory variants and explain trade-offs

sessions.title: Builder Pattern
objectives: Implement Builder with method chaining and validation
Build HTTP request and SQL query builders
Compare Builder vs telescoping constructors

activities.description: Build an HTTP request builder with validation
Build a SQL query builder that generates valid SELECT statements

reviewQuestions: Builder vs Factory?:::Factory decides WHICH class (type varies). Builder decides HOW to configure one class (options vary). Factory: 'email vs SMS notifier.' Builder: 'HTTP request with these headers and timeout.'

Why prefer Builder over telescoping constructors?:::Readability (.header('Auth', 'token') beats positional args), safety (validates in build()), flexibility (optional params without null), immutability (build() returns frozen object).
When is Builder unnecessary?:::Objects with 1-3 required params. Languages with named params (Kotlin, Python). Simple value objects where a constructor suffices.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement fluent Builder API with validation and explain trade-offs

sessions.title: Observer Pattern
objectives: Implement generic EventEmitter with on/off/emit
Build domain-specific Observer (stock exchange)
Understand Observer in React, Node.js, event-driven architectures

activities.description: Build a generic EventEmitter
Implement stock exchange with alert, bot, and audit observers

reviewQuestions: Observer vs Pub/Sub?:::Observer: subject holds direct references to observers (in-process). Pub/Sub: message broker mediates — publishers and subscribers never know each other. Pub/Sub works across processes; Observer is simpler in-process.

How to prevent memory leaks?:::Always unsubscribe when observer's lifecycle ends. React: cleanup in useEffect. Java: WeakReference lists. Android: lifecycle-aware LiveData. Node: emitter.removeListener().
Push vs Pull model?:::Push: data sent with notification (emit('price', {value: 150})). Pull: just notify 'changed', observer queries. Push is simpler; Pull avoids sending unneeded data.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement Observer/EventEmitter and explain its role in event-driven architecture

sessions.title: Strategy Pattern
objectives: Implement Strategy with runtime switching
Build sorting and payment strategy systems
Compare Strategy vs if/else, State, Template Method

activities.description: Implement sorting with 3 interchangeable strategies
Build payment checkout with Strategy pattern

reviewQuestions: Strategy vs if/else chains?:::Strategy replaces conditionals with polymorphism. Each algorithm is a separate testable class. Adding a new algorithm = new class, no modification of existing code (OCP). if/else chains grow linearly and violate OCP.

Strategy vs State pattern?:::Both change behavior via composition. Strategy: the CLIENT explicitly chooses which algorithm. State: the OBJECT transitions between states automatically based on internal logic.

When to use lambdas instead of Strategy classes?:::When strategies are simple (one method, few lines). Java 8+ Comparator.comparing() is a lambda strategy. Use full classes when strategies need state, multiple methods, or complex logic.
sessions.successCriteria: Can replace conditionals with Strategy and switch algorithms at runtime

sessions.title: Decorator Pattern
objectives: Implement composable Decorator chains
Understand OOP Decorator vs Python @decorator
Build logger and coffee shop examples

activities.description: Build a logger with composable decorators (timestamp, prefix, uppercase)
Implement Python @decorator for retry and timing

reviewQuestions: Decorator vs inheritance?:::Decorator adds behavior at runtime via composition — flexible, combinable. Inheritance is compile-time, creates tight coupling, and causes class explosion (N features = 2^N subclass combinations). Decorator follows OCP.

Decorator vs Proxy?:::Same wrapper structure, different intent. Decorator: enhance/extend behavior (add logging, caching). Proxy: control access (auth check, lazy loading). A caching proxy and caching decorator may look identical in code.

Does order matter in Decorator chains?:::Yes. Timestamp(UpperCase(Console)): message is uppercased first, then timestamped. UpperCase(Timestamp(Console)): timestamp is also uppercased. The outermost decorator runs first.
sessions.successCriteria: Can compose multiple Decorators and explain vs inheritance/Proxy

sessions.title: Adapter Pattern
objectives: Implement Adapter for legacy system integration
Build API response adapters
Distinguish Adapter from Facade and Decorator

activities.description: Adapt a legacy payment gateway to a modern interface
Build an API response adapter (snake_case to camelCase)

reviewQuestions: Adapter vs Facade?:::Adapter translates ONE interface to ANOTHER (1:1 mapping, same complexity). Facade simplifies a COMPLEX SUBSYSTEM behind one simple API (many:1, reduces complexity).

Class vs Object Adapter?:::Class adapter uses multiple inheritance (extends both interfaces). Object adapter uses composition (wraps adaptee). Object adapter is preferred — more flexible, avoids inheritance issues.
Adapter vs Decorator?:::Adapter CHANGES the interface (different types in/out). Decorator PRESERVES the interface (same types, adds behavior). Both wrap objects, but purpose differs.
sessions.successCriteria: Can adapt incompatible interfaces and explain Adapter vs Facade/Decorator

sessions.title: Facade Pattern
objectives: Implement Facade for complex multi-step workflows
Build order processing and analytics facades
Distinguish Facade from Adapter and Mediator

activities.description: Build an order processing Facade with error handling
Build an analytics Facade that sends to multiple providers

reviewQuestions: Facade vs Adapter?:::Facade SIMPLIFIES (many subsystems behind one API, reduces complexity). Adapter TRANSLATES (one interface to another, same complexity). Facade is many-to-one; Adapter is one-to-one.
Can Facade become a God Object?:::Yes, if it orchestrates too many unrelated concerns. Keep facades focused: OrderFacade, AnalyticsFacade, PaymentFacade — not a single AppFacade that does everything.
Facade in microservices?:::API Gateway is a Facade — single entry point that routes requests to multiple backend services. BFF (Backend for Frontend) is also a specialized Facade.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design clean Facade APIs for complex subsystem orchestration

sessions.title: Command Pattern
objectives: Implement undo/redo system with Command pattern
Build text editor with insert and delete commands
Understand Command in event sourcing and Redux

activities.description: Build a text editor with full undo/redo using Command
Add a macro command that replays a sequence

reviewQuestions: Command vs Strategy?:::Both encapsulate behavior. Strategy: choose ONE algorithm, swap at runtime. Command: store MANY operations in history, support undo/redo/queue. Strategy answers 'how?'; Command answers 'what happened?'

How does Command enable undo?:::Each command stores enough state to reverse itself. InsertCommand stores the text inserted; undo() deletes that exact text. CommandHistory maintains a stack of executed commands.

Command vs Memento for undo?:::Command stores the DELTA (what changed). Memento stores the full STATE SNAPSHOT. Command is memory-efficient; Memento is simpler for complex state. Most real systems use Command.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement full undo/redo system and explain Command vs Strategy

sessions.title: Proxy Pattern
objectives: Implement caching, logging, and auth proxies
Stack multiple proxies for combined behavior
Distinguish Proxy from Decorator

activities.description: Build caching proxy for database queries
Add auth proxy and stack with caching + logging

reviewQuestions: Proxy vs Decorator?:::Same wrapper structure. Proxy controls ACCESS (auth, caching, lazy loading). Decorator enhances BEHAVIOR (add logging, retry). Proxy often manages the real object's lifecycle; Decorator receives the object to wrap.

Types of Proxy?:::Caching (stores results), Access Control (auth checks), Logging (audit), Lazy Loading (defer initialization), Remote (local rep of remote object), Virtual (placeholder for expensive object).

How does Spring use Proxy?:::@Transactional wraps methods in proxy managing DB transactions. @Cacheable adds caching proxy. Uses JDK dynamic proxies (interface-based) or CGLIB (class-based subclassing).
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement multiple proxy types and stack them for combined behavior

sessions.title: MVC / MVVM / Clean Architecture
objectives: Implement Clean Architecture with Entity, UseCase, Repository, Controller layers
Compare MVC, MVVM, and Clean Architecture
Apply the Dependency Rule correctly

activities.description: Build a user management system with Clean Architecture layers
Swap InMemoryRepo for a different implementation without changing use cases

reviewQuestions: When Clean Architecture over MVC?:::Clean for complex domains with business rules that must be independently testable and framework-agnostic. MVC for simple CRUD where the extra layers add unnecessary abstraction. If your app is mostly forms and database, MVC is fine.

What is the Dependency Rule?:::Dependencies always point INWARD. Entities (core) know nothing about Use Cases. Use Cases know nothing about Controllers. Outer layers depend on inner layers, never reverse. This makes the core testable without any infrastructure.

MVVM vs MVC?:::MVC: Controller explicitly pushes data to View (imperative). MVVM: ViewModel exposes observable state, View automatically reacts via data binding (declarative). MVVM reduces boilerplate and is preferred for rich UIs (Angular, SwiftUI).
sessions.successCriteria: Can structure a project with Clean Architecture and explain when to use MVC/MVVM instead

sessions.title: Repository Pattern
objectives: Implement Repository with InMemory and SQL backends
Build a service that uses Repository without knowing the implementation
Understand Repository in DDD and Clean Architecture

activities.description: Build UserRepository with InMemory and SQL implementations
Write a UserService that works identically with either repo

reviewQuestions: Why not call DB directly?:::Direct calls couple business logic to specific database technology. Impossible to unit test without real DB. Violates DIP. Repository isolates data access behind an interface — swap Postgres for InMemory in tests.

Repository vs DAO?:::DAO is data-access focused (CRUD for one table). Repository is domain-focused (may aggregate data from multiple tables/sources). Repository speaks domain language (findActive, findByEmail), not SQL.

What is the Generic Repository anti-pattern?:::A single Repository<T> with only save/delete/findById. Too generic — no domain-specific queries, leads to leaky abstractions. Better: domain-specific repos with meaningful methods.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement Repository with multiple backends and explain its role in Clean Architecture

sessions.title: Event Sourcing & CQRS
objectives: Implement Event Sourcing with event store and replay
Build CQRS read model projections
Understand trade-offs vs traditional CRUD

activities.description: Build an event-sourced bank account with deposit/withdraw/balance
Create a read-model projection (account summary view)

reviewQuestions: Event Sourcing vs CRUD?:::CRUD overwrites state — you lose history. ES stores immutable events — complete audit trail, temporal queries ('what was balance on Jan 15?'). Trade-off: ES is more complex, requires replay, but gives full traceability.

What is eventual consistency in CQRS?:::Read model may lag behind write model because events propagate asynchronously. Fine for dashboards and reports; problematic when users expect immediate consistency (e.g., seeing their just-placed order).

What are snapshots in Event Sourcing?:::For aggregates with thousands of events, replaying all is slow. Snapshots save the full state periodically. Replay starts from last snapshot + only newer events. Optimization, not a requirement.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement event store with state replay and build CQRS read projections

sessions.title: Anti-Patterns & Code Smells
objectives: Recognize 5 anti-patterns and 5 code smells
Refactor anti-pattern code to clean code
Know when and how to refactor

activities.description: Identify anti-patterns in code samples and refactor them
Code review exercise: find all code smells in a class

reviewQuestions: Name 3 code smells and their fixes.:::(1) Long Method (>20 lines) -> Extract smaller methods. (2) Feature Envy (method uses another class's data more than its own) -> Move method to that class. (3) Duplicate Code (same logic in 2+ places) -> Extract to shared function or base class.

What is Primitive Obsession?:::Using primitive types (String, int) for domain concepts. 'String email' should be 'Email email' — a value object that validates format, prevents invalid data, and centralizes logic. Same for Money, PhoneNumber, Address.

What is the Distributed Monolith anti-pattern?:::Microservices that share a database, must deploy together, and can't function independently. You get ALL the complexity of microservices with NONE of the benefits. Fix: bounded contexts, separate databases, async communication.
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify anti-patterns in any codebase and propose concrete refactoring steps

quizBank.question: Which SOLID principle does Factory support?
options: A) SRP
B) Open/Closed
C) LSP
D) DIP
quizBank.explanation: Factory lets you add new types without modifying existing code.

quizBank.question: Decorator vs inheritance?
quizBank.explanation: Decorator adds behavior at runtime without modifying the class. Inheritance is compile-time and creates tight coupling.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use Singleton?
quizBank.explanation: When testability matters (global state is hard to mock), when multiple instances might be needed, or when DI is available.

quizBank.question: What is CQRS?
quizBank.explanation: Separate read and write models. Writes go through command handlers to an event store. Reads use optimized materialized views.

quizBank.question: Identify the anti-pattern: class ReportManager handles auth, data fetching, PDF generation, and email.
quizBank.explanation: God Object. Split into AuthService, DataService, ReportGenerator, EmailService.

quizBank.question: What is the key structural difference between Decorator and Proxy patterns?
options: A) Decorator wraps objects to add behavior; Proxy wraps objects to control access
B) Proxy adds new methods; Decorator only modifies existing ones
C) Decorator is used at compile time; Proxy is used at runtime
D) There is no difference — they are identical patterns
quizBank.explanation: Both patterns use a wrapper that implements the same interface as the wrapped object. The intent differs: Decorator enhances or extends behavior (adds logging, retry, caching to a method). Proxy controls access to the real object (authentication, lazy initialization, remote access). In practice, a caching proxy and a caching decorator look identical in code — the distinction is conceptual purpose. Use Decorator when the focus is feature enhancement; use Proxy when the focus is access control or lifecycle management.

quizBank.question: Which scenario is the Command pattern BEST suited for?
options: A) Choosing between sorting algorithms at runtime
B) Providing a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
C) Implementing undo/redo functionality in a text editor
D) Converting one interface to another for legacy integration
quizBank.explanation: Command encapsulates a request as an object with execute() and undo() methods. This enables undo/redo stacks (text editors, Photoshop), request queuing (job systems, task schedulers), and transaction logging (event sourcing). Choice A is Strategy. Choice B is Facade. Choice D is Adapter. The hallmark of Command is that the request itself becomes a first-class object that can be stored, queued, replayed, or reversed.

quizBank.question: In Clean Architecture, which layer is at the innermost core and has ZERO dependencies on outer layers?
options: A) Interface Adapters (Controllers, Presenters)
B) Frameworks & Drivers (Express, PostgreSQL, React)
C) Use Cases (Application Business Rules)
D) Entities (Enterprise Business Rules)
quizBank.explanation: Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule states that source code dependencies must always point inward. Entities are the innermost layer — pure business objects with no knowledge of databases, frameworks, or delivery mechanisms. Use Cases (application logic) depend only on Entities. Interface Adapters (controllers, repositories) depend on Use Cases. Frameworks & Drivers are the outermost layer. This makes Entities fully testable with zero infrastructure.

quizBank.question: A developer builds an OrderService that directly instantiates MySQLDatabase, SMTPEmailer, and StripePayment inside its methods. Which anti-pattern does this represent?
options: A) Golden Hammer
B) God Object
C) Tight Coupling / Dependency Inversion violation
D) Premature Optimization
quizBank.explanation: Directly instantiating concrete dependencies inside a class violates the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) — high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concretions. OrderService becomes tightly coupled to MySQL, SMTP, and Stripe — untestable, unswappable, and fragile. The fix is Dependency Injection: accept IDatabase, IEmailer, IPayment interfaces in the constructor. God Object is one class doing too many unrelated things. Golden Hammer is using one tool for every problem.

quizBank.question: What is the key difference between Simple Factory and Abstract Factory?
options: A) Simple Factory uses static methods; Abstract Factory cannot
B) Abstract Factory creates families of related objects; Simple Factory creates one type of object
C) Simple Factory is a design pattern; Abstract Factory is just a programming technique
D) Abstract Factory always uses inheritance; Simple Factory uses composition
quizBank.explanation: Simple Factory: one factory creates one product type — e.g., NotificationFactory.create('email' | 'sms' | 'push'). Abstract Factory: creates families of related products — e.g., UIFactory creates Button + Input + Modal as a coherent set (DarkThemeFactory vs LightThemeFactory). Abstract Factory enforces consistency across related objects; Simple Factory just encapsulates creation logic. Abstract Factory is the formal GoF pattern; Simple Factory is a useful programming idiom, not an official GoF pattern.

interviewTips: Don't over-engineer — simplest pattern that works
Be ready to draw UML diagrams
Composition over inheritance — always mention this
